class_schedule.topic: Digital Journalism in the Post-Print Era
learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of journalism from print to digital-native models
Identify the core principles of journalism that persist regardless of platform
Evaluate the economic models sustaining digital journalism today
readings: Anderson, Bell & Shirky, Post-Industrial Journalism (Tow Center report)
Columbia Journalism Review, 'The New Economics of Journalism'

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital journalism is not a different kind of journalism. It is journalism practiced with digital tools, for digital audiences, on digital platforms. The core principles, accuracy, fairness, independence, and public service, remain constant. The methods, formats, and business models have transformed.
key_concepts: Journalism fundamentals: accuracy, verification, independence, accountability, public interest
Digital-native journalism: organizations built for the web first (Axios, The Verge, Defector)
Revenue models: advertising, subscriptions, memberships, donations, events, licensing
Platform dependency: the tension between distributing journalism on social platforms and owning the audience relationship
Audience analytics: using data to inform editorial decisions without letting clicks dictate coverage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing how the same story is covered by a legacy newspaper website, a digital-native publication, and a social-first news brand, analyzing differences in format, depth, and audience engagement
setup_requirements: Subscriptions or free access to 3-5 news outlets for course reading
RSS reader or news aggregator configured

review.value: The journalistic imperative to verify all facts before publishing; in the digital speed culture, the pressure to be first must never override the obligation to be right
Building audience on social platforms means a single algorithm change can destroy your distribution; smart digital publishers build owned channels (email, apps, websites) alongside social presence

class_schedule.topic: Reporting and Verification in the Digital Age
learning_objectives: Apply digital verification techniques to online sources, images, and documents
Conduct research using digital tools: databases, social media, public records
Evaluate source credibility in an era of misinformation and synthetic media

lecture_notes.introduction: The internet is the greatest reporting tool ever created and the greatest source of misinformation ever created. A digital journalist must be skilled at using it for both research and verification, always treating online information as a starting point for verification, not a substitute for it.
key_concepts: Digital verification toolkit: reverse image search, EXIF data analysis, geolocation, source triangulation
Public records research: government databases, court records, corporate filings accessible online
Social media as source: finding sources, verifying identities, and ethically using social media content
Source evaluation hierarchy: primary sources > official records > credible secondary sources > social media
Deepfake and AI content detection: tools and techniques for identifying synthetic media
lecture_notes.demonstration: Verification exercise: students receive five pieces of viral online content (images, claims, documents) and must verify or debunk each using digital tools within a 30-minute deadline
setup_requirements: Verification toolkit: Google Reverse Image Search, TinEye, InVID, CrowdTangle

review.value: Confirming information through at least three independent sources before reporting; if only one source makes a claim, it is a tip worth investigating, not a fact worth publishing

Metadata embedded in digital images containing camera model, date taken, GPS coordinates, and editing software; used to verify when, where, and how a photo was actually taken, though it can be stripped or altered

learning_objectives: Adapt news stories for different digital platforms while maintaining journalistic standards
Create multimedia journalism packages combining text, images, video, and interactive elements
Apply data journalism techniques to enhance reporting with quantitative evidence
readings: Knight Foundation, 'Future of News' research brief

lecture_notes.introduction: A modern digital journalist does not write a story and hand it to a designer. They conceive stories as multimedia experiences from the start, choosing the medium that best serves each element of the narrative: text for analysis, video for emotion, data visualization for evidence, and interactive elements for exploration.
key_concepts: Multimedia journalism: combining text, photography, video, audio, graphics, and interactive elements
Platform-native news content: Twitter/X threads for breaking news, Instagram for visual stories, TikTok for explainers
Data journalism: using quantitative data analysis as a reporting tool and creating visualizations that reveal patterns
Interactive storytelling: scrollytelling, interactive maps, and exploratory data tools for audience engagement
Audience-first format selection: choosing media types based on what serves the audience and the story, not the journalist's preference
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a multi-platform journalism package for a local story: writing the web article, creating an Instagram visual story, producing a 60-second TikTok explainer, and designing a data visualization, all from the same reporting
setup_requirements: Smartphone for photo and video capture
Data visualization tool (Datawrapper or Flourish free tier)

review.value: A digital narrative format where the story unfolds as the reader scrolls, with text, images, video, and interactive elements appearing in response to scroll position; effective for immersive longform journalism that would lose readers as a traditional article

Using quantitative data analysis as a primary reporting method: finding stories in datasets, verifying claims with data, and creating visualizations that make complex information accessible and compelling for audiences

learning_objectives: Build and engage news audiences through digital channels and community interaction
Apply newsletter journalism as a direct audience relationship tool
Navigate the ethical boundaries of audience engagement in journalism

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital journalism is not a one-way broadcast. The most successful digital newsrooms build relationships with their audiences through newsletters, comments, social media, and community events. Engagement builds loyalty, trust, and sustainability, but it must not compromise editorial independence.
key_concepts: Newsletter journalism: email newsletters as direct audience channels free from algorithmic gatekeeping
Community journalism: serving defined communities with relevant, localized coverage
Audience engagement ethics: maintaining editorial independence while being responsive to audience interests
Comment moderation and community standards: fostering productive discussion while managing toxicity
Membership models: audiences who pay to support journalism they value, not just access it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three successful journalism newsletters (Morning Brew, The Hustle, local news examples), deconstructing their format, voice, engagement tactics, and business model
setup_requirements: Substack or similar free newsletter platform

review.value: Email newsletters give journalists direct access to their audience without social media algorithms as intermediaries; the journalist controls distribution, format, and frequency, building a relationship that survives platform changes

Engaging with audiences and responding to their interests is good journalism; letting audience preferences dictate coverage decisions is not. The journalist serves the public interest, informed by audience feedback but not governed by it

class_schedule.topic: Ethics and Law for Digital Journalists
learning_objectives: Apply the SPJ Code of Ethics to digital journalism scenarios
Navigate legal issues specific to digital journalism: shield laws, SLAPP suits, and online defamation
Evaluate the ethical implications of AI tools in journalism
readings: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, 'Digital Media Law' guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital journalism raises ethical and legal questions that traditional journalism never faced: Is it ethical to use a public social media post without permission? Can you be sued for a tweet? How should you disclose AI assistance in reporting? This session addresses the unique ethical and legal landscape of digital journalism.
key_concepts: SPJ ethics applied to digital: truth (verification), minimize harm (considering online amplification), act independently, be accountable
Social media ethics: using public posts in reporting, protecting sources who contact you through social media
Shield laws: journalist privilege to protect sources, and whether digital journalists qualify
Defamation in digital contexts: tweets, social media posts, and the permanent nature of digital publication
AI in journalism: ethical use for research, data analysis, and content creation with mandatory disclosure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethics case study analysis: examining three digital journalism ethics dilemmas (using a private Facebook post in a story, live-tweeting a sensitive event, and using AI to analyze leaked documents) with structured ethical reasoning
setup_requirements: Legal reference guide for digital journalists

review.value: In digital journalism, publishing a story amplifies it to a potentially unlimited audience; the ethical obligation to minimize harm must account for the scale of digital distribution, which is fundamentally different from a local newspaper

Legal protections allowing journalists to refuse to reveal sources vary by state and often were written before digital media existed; whether bloggers, newsletter writers, and social media journalists qualify is an evolving legal question

questions.question: Explain the concept of source triangulation and describe why it is more important in digital journalism than in traditional print journalism.
questions.answer: Source triangulation means confirming information through at least three independent sources before publishing. It is more important in digital journalism because: (1) The speed of digital publication creates pressure to publish quickly, increasing the temptation to skip verification. (2) Online sources are easier to fabricate (fake websites, fake social media accounts, AI-generated content). (3) Digital publication is permanent and instantly shareable; errors spread globally in minutes and cannot be recalled like a print run. (4) The abundance of online information creates an illusion of confirmation when multiple sources are actually citing the same unverified original.

questions.question: What is platform dependency risk for digital news organizations, and describe one strategy to mitigate it.
questions.answer: Platform dependency risk is the vulnerability created when a news organization relies primarily on social media platforms for audience reach. A single algorithm change (Facebook reducing news content in feeds, Twitter/X changing distribution rules) can instantly reduce traffic by 50% or more without warning or recourse. Mitigation strategy: build an email newsletter as a direct audience channel. Email is an owned channel where the news organization controls the subscriber list, distribution timing, and content format without algorithmic intermediaries. Even if every social platform changes its algorithm, the newsletter audience remains accessible. This is why many digital journalists prioritize newsletter subscriber growth alongside social media following.

questions.question: What is data journalism, and describe how a journalist would use data analysis to enhance a story about local housing affordability.
questions.answer: Data journalism uses quantitative data analysis as a primary reporting method: finding stories in datasets, verifying claims with data, and creating visualizations. For housing affordability: a journalist would obtain public datasets (Census Bureau income data, Zillow/Redfin housing price data, building permit records) and analyze trends such as median home price vs. median income ratio over time, new construction rates vs. population growth, and geographic distribution of affordable vs. unaffordable areas. The resulting data visualization (interactive map showing affordability by neighborhood, chart showing the widening gap over a decade) provides evidence that makes the story concrete and inarguable, going beyond anecdotes to systemic patterns.

rubric.key: Identifies specific datasets and analysis for the scenario
Describes how visualization enhances the story

questions.question: How should a journalist ethically handle using a public social media post as a source in a news story?
questions.answer: Ethical handling requires: (1) Verify the post is authentic and the account belongs to who it appears to be. (2) Consider whether the poster understood their content could become news; a casual personal post is different from a public statement by a public figure. (3) Attempt to contact the poster for comment and consent before publication, especially for private individuals. (4) Consider whether publishing the post could cause harm to the poster (doxxing, harassment, retaliation). (5) Attribute the post clearly with context about when and where it was posted. (6) Consider whether the newsworthiness justifies the potential invasion of privacy. Public does not automatically mean fair game; the SPJ code requires journalists to minimize harm while seeking truth.

rubric.key: Considers potential harm to the poster

major_assignments.title: Digital Journalism Package
major_assignments.description: Produce a complete multi-platform journalism package on a local story. The package must include original reporting with verified sources, a web-ready article, a social media story package, a data visualization, and a newsletter edition. All work must adhere to SPJ ethical standards.
objectives: Conduct original reporting with verified sources and ethical practices
Produce a web-ready article meeting professional journalism standards
Create multi-platform content adapting the story for social and newsletter formats
Apply data journalism techniques to enhance the reporting
deliverables: Web-ready article (800-1200 words) with original reporting and verified sources
Social media story package: Instagram/TikTok visual story or Twitter/X thread
Data visualization supporting the story (created in Datawrapper or Flourish)
Newsletter edition featuring the story with appropriate format and voice
Source log documenting all sources, verification steps, and ethical considerations

criteria: Story has clear news value and public interest justification
Sources are multiple, independent, and properly verified
Reporting demonstrates genuine investigation beyond surface-level information
Ethical standards are maintained throughout
Article is well-structured, clear, and engaging
Writing meets professional journalism standards
Facts are presented accurately with proper attribution
Headline and lead are compelling and honest
Social media content is platform-native and journalistically sound
Data visualization adds genuine informational value
Newsletter edition demonstrates appropriate format adaptation
All platforms maintain consistent accuracy and ethical standards
Source log is comprehensive and detailed
Verification steps are documented for each key claim
Ethical considerations are identified and addressed
Source diversity demonstrates thorough reporting
Strong original reporting with verified sources
Professional writing meeting journalism standards
Effective multi-platform adaptation maintaining accuracy
Thorough source documentation demonstrating ethical practice

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on journalism ethics, verification, and digital practices

weekly_assignments.description: Weekly reporting, writing, and verification exercises

final_portfolio.description: Final journalism portfolio demonstrating growth across the semester

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions. Journalism is time-sensitive and many exercises cannot be replicated. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Journalism has deadlines. Late assignments lose 15% per day. This is higher than other courses because deadline discipline is a core professional competency.
course_policies.collaboration: Reporting may involve source sharing with instructor approval. All writing and production is individual. Plagiarism, fabrication, or misattribution is grounds for course failure.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Journalistic integrity is the highest standard. Fabricating sources, quotes, or facts results in automatic course failure. All sources must be real and accurately represented.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a smartphone for photo/video, a laptop for writing, and access to Datawrapper, Canva, and a newsletter platform. Recording equipment is available from the media lab.
